perturbation.original_text: The Parties expressly intend and agree that CONSULTANT is acting as an independent contractor and not as an employee of REIT. Under no circumstances shall CONSULTANT look to REIT as its employer, or as a partner, agent, or principal. CONSULTANT has no right or authority to act as an agent of REIT or for or on behalf of the Company in any capacity whatsoever or to assume or create any obligation of any kind - express or implied - on behalf of REIT.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties expressly intend and agree that CONSULTANT is acting as an independent contractor and not as an employee of REIT. Under no circumstances shall CONSULTANT look to REIT as its employer, or as a partner, agent, or principal. CONSULTANT has the right and authority to act as an agent of REIT, but only with written permission from the CEO. The Consultant cannot assume or create any obligation of any kind - express or implied - on behalf of REIT.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text initially states the consultant is an independent contractor, but then grants them agency authority with CEO permission. This creates a contradiction because independent contractors generally do not have the authority to act as agents unless specifically outlined by common law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Agency Law - specifically apparent authority which is often defined by state common law derived from the Restatement (Third) of Agency
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows the consultant to act as an agent, contradicting the original intention of an independent contractor relationship. Apparent authority, under agency law, arises from a principal's (REIT) manifestations to a third party, leading that party to reasonably believe that the agent (Consultant) has authority. If REIT allows the consultant to act as an agent even with CEO permission, it creates apparent authority, blurring the lines of the independent contractor agreement and potentially creating liability for REIT.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . [Last reviewed in October of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS trial process/advocacy COMMERCE LIFE EVENTS family & personal matters elder law juvenile law wills PROPERTY trusts inheritances & estates THE LEGAL PROCESS class actions courts criminal procedure legal practice/ethics wex articles business law agency wex definitions business sectors civil procedure family law individual rights legal education and practice property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it initially defines the consultant as an independent contractor but then grants them agency authority with CEO permission. This contradicts the fundamental nature of an independent contractor relationship. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, "A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal." By granting the consultant agency authority, even with CEO permission, the modified text blurs the lines between an independent contractor and an agent, potentially creating liability for REIT. The law snippet defines express authority as "An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests." This agency authority, even if limited, contradicts the independent contractor status initially established in the agreement and could lead to misinterpretations by third parties, as discussed in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Termination for Cause" shall mean termination of this Agreement by the Company as the result of: (i) any act of fraud, dishonesty or neglect of services by CONSULTANT in connection with the services to be provided under this Agreement or against any Company customer, vendor or affiliated company; or (ii) the breach or prospective breach of any provision of this Agreement by CONSULTANT.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Termination for Cause" shall mean termination of this Agreement by the Company as the result of: (i) any act of fraud, dishonesty or neglect of services by CONSULTANT in connection with the services to be provided under this Agreement or against any Company customer, vendor or affiliated company; or (ii) the breach or prospective breach of any provision of this Agreement by CONSULTANT; or (iii) the company's dissatisfaction with the consultant's performance.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "the company's dissatisfaction with the consultant's performance" to the definition of 'Termination for Cause' creates ambiguity. While 'fraud,' 'dishonesty,' and 'neglect' have some established legal meaning, 'dissatisfaction' is subjective and can be interpreted broadly. This contradicts the idea of 'for cause' which usually requires specific, justifiable reasons.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contract law. While Virginia law is referenced, the UCC reflects fundamental contract principles widely adopted. Allowing termination based solely on 'dissatisfaction' without objective criteria enables arbitrary termination, undermining the consultant's reasonable expectation of the contract's benefits and potentially violating the implied duty of good faith. Some jurisdictions require objective standards even within subjective satisfaction clauses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The added clause "the company's dissatisfaction with the consultant's performance" directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract defined 'Termination for Cause' with specific, objective reasons like fraud, dishonesty, or breach of contract. The modification introduces a subjective element ('dissatisfaction') allowing termination without a justifiable reason, potentially undermining the consultant's expectation of the contract's benefits. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Allowing termination based solely on 'dissatisfaction' violates this obligation, as it enables arbitrary termination.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT agrees that during its consultancy for REIT and for a period of twelve (12) months immediately following the termination of its consultancy with the Company for any reason, whether with or without cause, it will not: (a) solicit, entice or induce any Customer for the purpose of providing, or provide, products or services that are competitive with the products or services provided by the Company, or (b) solicit, entice, or induce any Customer to terminate or reduce its business with (or refrain from increasing its business with) the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT agrees that during its consultancy for REIT and for a period of twelve (12) months immediately following the termination of its consultancy with the Company for any reason, whether with or without cause, it will not: (a) solicit, entice or induce any Customer for the purpose of providing, or provide, products or services that are competitive with the products or services provided by the Company unless pre-approved by the Company's Board of Directors, or (b) solicit, entice, or induce any Customer to terminate or reduce its business with (or refrain from increasing its business with) the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'unless pre-approved by the Company's Board of Directors' to the non-solicitation clause creates uncertainty. It suggests the non-solicitation agreement can be waived, potentially making it unenforceable due to lack of consistent application. This undermines the purpose of a non-solicitation agreement, which is to protect the company's customer relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Restraint of Trade Statute (Virginia Code § 59.1-9.5)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Virginia permits reasonable non-compete/non-solicitation agreements, they must be carefully tailored to protect legitimate business interests. Adding a discretionary waiver ('unless pre-approved by the Company's Board of Directors') introduces an element of arbitrariness. If the board frequently waives the restriction, it weakens the argument that the restriction is necessary to protect legitimate business interests, potentially rendering it unenforceable under Virginia Code § 59.1-9.5 because it demonstrates the restriction is not consistently applied and may not be genuinely necessary. The modified text undermines the predictability and enforceability required for such covenants.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a discretionary waiver to the non-solicitation agreement ('unless pre-approved by the Company's Board of Directors'), potentially making it unenforceable. The Law Explanation argues this undermines the consistent application required for such covenants, which is necessary to protect legitimate business interests under Virginia Code § 59.1-9.5. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every contract, combination or conspiracy in restraint of trade or commerce of this Commonwealth is unlawful.' While the law doesn't directly forbid waivers, the Law Explanation suggests that inconsistent application of a non-solicitation clause weakens the argument that it's a reasonable restraint of trade, creating a contradiction where the modified clause could be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade due to its discretionary nature.
